en.extract:
The spinifexbird (Poodytes carteri) is endemic to inland Australia. Also known as Carter's desertbird, it is named after Thomas Carter, an English ornithologist and pastoralist active in Western Australia from 1887 to 1928.


Description
It has a rich brown cap, golden brown streaked wings, and a long tail. Both sexes are alike.


Behaviour
Its diet comprises a variety of insects and seeds collected in "spinifex" or Triodia grass. This species flies weakly, with its tail drooping. It tends to be solitary and sedentary. The breeding season of the spinifexbird stretches from August to November. Its nest is a shallow cup built in clumps of Triodia grass close to the ground, usually with a clutch of two eggs. Not globally threatened, the species may be common in suitable habitats, although it is rarely seen due to the remote and arid nature of its habitat.

''Megalurus carteri''}} The '''spinifexbird''' ('''''Poodytes carteri''''') is endemic to inland [[Australia]]. Also known as '''Carter's desertbird''', it is named after [[Thomas Carter (ornithologist)|Thomas Carter]], an English ornithologist and pastoralist active in Western Australia from 1887 to 1928.{{cite web|last1=McCarthy|first1=G.J.|last2=Walker|first2=Rosanne|title=Carter, Thomas (1863 - 1931) |url=http://www.eoas.info/biogs/P000933b.htm|website=Encyclopedia of Australian Science|publisher=eScholarship Research Centre, The University of Melbourne|access-date=23 February 2018|date=2017}} ==Description== It has a rich brown cap, golden brown streaked wings, and a long [[tail]]. Both sexes are alike. ==Behaviour== Its diet comprises a variety of [[insects]] and [[seeds]] collected in "spinifex" or ''[[Triodia (grass)|Triodia]]'' grass. This species flies weakly, with its tail drooping. It tends to be solitary and sedentary. The breeding season of the spinifexbird stretches from August to November. Its nest is a shallow cup built in clumps of ''Triodia'' grass close to the ground, usually with a clutch of two [[egg (biology)|eggs]]. Not globally threatened, the species may be common in suitable habitats, although it is rarely seen due to the remote and arid nature of its [[habitat]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1301366}} [[Category:Poodytes]] [[Category:Endemic birds of Australia]] [[Category:Birds described in 1900]] [[Category:Taxa named by Alfred John North]] [[Category:Taxonomy articles created by Polbot]] {{Locustellidae-stub}}
